summary: Gruber translates Macrovision CEO Fred Amoroso's official response to Steve Jobs's 'Thoughts on Music' open letter, revealing the self-interested corporate motives behind each piece of PR language. The post systematically deconstructs Macrovision's defense of DRM by restating each paragraph in blunt, honest terms. Gruber exposes that Macrovision's arguments for DRM are fundamentally about preserving its own business model rather than serving consumers or content creators. The translation reveals the absurdity of claiming DRM increases consumer value, that interoperable DRM is achievable, and that content owners would abandon digital distribution without copy protection. The piece is a masterclass in cutting through corporate doublespeak to expose naked self-interest.
key_points: Macrovision's defense of DRM is entirely motivated by protecting its own business as a copy protection vendor, not by consumer or industry benefit
The concept of 'interoperable DRM' is presented as a pipe dream that gives people none of the actual benefits of DRM-free media while enriching middlemen
Macrovision's offer to 'help' Apple with FairPlay is really an insult, implying Apple can't handle its own technology
The claim that abandoning DRM would increase consumer costs inverts reality — DRM exists to charge people repeatedly for content they already own
Macrovision's argument that content owners would flee digital distribution without DRM ignores the massive market incentives to sell digitally
money_quotes: We've been helping and encouraging the entertainment industry to annoy its paying customers for more than 20 years.
Remember those squiggly lines when you tried copying a commercial VHS tape? You can thank us for that.
We recognize that if getting rid of DRM works for the music industry, it's going to open the eyes of executives in other fields, and it could unravel Macrovision's entire business.
Up is down. Black is white.
The solution is more DRM. DRM everywhere.
Abandoning DRM will prevent us from forcing our customers to keep paying us over and over again for the same movies and songs they've already paid for.
I am high as a kite.

Just because we have sold the entertainment industry on the pipe dream of 'interoperable DRM' that can't actually be implemented does not mean they should stop paying Macrovision in a futile attempt to make it happen.
key_insight: Macrovision's defense of DRM is a transparent exercise in corporate self-preservation disguised as consumer advocacy, and translating PR-speak into plain English instantly exposes the absurdity.
